R typically captures a quantity like generalization or pop-
ulation test error (Hastie et al., 2022; Dobriban &Wager,
2018; Adlam & Pennington, 2020a; Bach, 2024), adversar-
ial robustness error (Scetbon & Dohmatob, 2023), out-of-
distribution error (Tripuraneni et al., 2021), or subgroup
test error (Subramonian et al., 2025). The sought-for limit
r is often considered in a regime where the dimensions
of the rectangular matrices diverge at given proportionate
rates, e.g., d/n  ПҶ, m/n  ПҲ, where d is the input di-

mension, n is the sample size, and m is the width of the
neural network. To an ML theorist, constants like ПҶ, ПҲ, and
О» play an analogous role to the constants that appear in the
standard model of physics (e.g., mass of electron, charge
of electron). A theorist can analyze r in terms of constants
like ПҶ, ПҲ, and О» to obtain fundamental insights into ML.
In practice, the limit r can be precisely computed analyt-
ically using the framework of operator-valued free proba-
bility theory (FPT) (Mingo & Speicher, 2017), which is at
the intersection of random matrix theory (RMT) and func-
tional analysis. An alternative approach is via replica sym-
metry (RS) calculations. Though analytically powerful, RS
is not rigorous and its proper use in ML theory requires

works have used operator-valued FPT to analyze the ex-
pected behavior of ML models in appropriate asymptotic
scaling limits, e.g., the rate of features d to samples n con-

theoretically analyze  the double descent  phenomenon
(Spigler et al., 2019; Belkin et al., 2019)  in ridge regres-
sion with random projections in a proportionate scaling

auto-fpt: Automating Free Probability Theory
some training in statistical physics.  In comparison, FPT
only requires knowledge of linear algebra and statistics.
Main contributions.  We  introduce and document  a
lightweight Python-based tool called auto-fpt for auto-
matically computing r symbolically, as a function of all the
relevant constants. We further showhow to use auto-fpt
to compute the high-dimensional: (1) Stieltjes transform of
the Marchenko-Pastur law, (2) generalization error of clas-
sical ridge regression, (3) sum of two sample covariance
matrices (subordination), and (4) training error of a random
features model. Our tool takes as input a linear pencil for
R and outputs a reduced system of п¬Ғxed-point equations
that can be solved to obtain r. We introduce various tech-
niques to make the computation of these equations more
tractable, especially for large pencils, e.g., matrix scalariza-
tion, sparse block matrix inversion, and duplicate equation
identiп¬Ғcation and pruning. auto-fpt currently assumes
that the randommatrices are Gaussian with IID entries and
the deterministic matrices commute.
A primary goal of auto-fpt is to make the machinery of
FPT more accessible to ML theorists and practitioners, so
that they can focus on other important aspects of the the-
oretical analysis of ML models, rather than tedious case-
by-case algebraic manipulations. To the best of our knowl-
edge, there do not exist any open-access tools that automat-
ically symbolically compute the high-dimensional limiting
value of the expected normalized trace of rational expres-
sions from their pencils (see Section 2). The only depen-
dencies required by auto-fpt are SymPy (Meurer et al.,
2017) and NumPy (Harris et al., 2020), both of which are
well-established open-source Python libraries. We hope
that theML community leverages auto-fpt to reproduce
known and uncover new phenomena in ML that contribute
to our foundational understanding of phenomena like gener-
alization, neural scaling laws, feature learning, memoriza-
tion, grokking, model collapse, and algorithmic fairness, in
solvable regimes which are relevant to neural networks and
large language models. This is especially important in the
context of emerging practices (e.g., training on synthetic
data, using attention, adapting to human feedback).

We  present  auto-fpt,  a  lightweight  Python-based
tool for automatically symbolically computing the high-
dimensional expected normalized trace of rational func-
tions from their pencils, which is a common procedure
in ML theory. We introduce various techniques to make
this computation more tractable, especially for large pen-
cils, e.g., matrix scalarization, sparse block matrix inver-
sion, and duplicate equation identiп¬Ғcation and pruning. We
show how to use auto-fpt to recover known theoretical
results. While we do not present new theoretical results,
a preliminary version of auto-fpt was used to guide
the derivation of the theories in Subramonian et al. (2025);
Dohmatob et al. (2025).
auto-fpt offers numerous promising future directions.
The runtime and tractability of the tool can be empirically
stress-tested with respect to rational expression size, pen-
cil size, and numerical stability.  In addition, the code can
be proп¬Ғled to identify performance bottlenecks. Moreover,
user studies can be run with ML theorists (both RMT ex-
perts and those who are unfamiliar with FPT) to under-
stand the efп¬Ғcacy and limitations of auto-fpt for their
research and deriving novel theoretical results (e.g., analyz-
ing two-layer neural networks beyond initialization). For
example, by default, auto-fpt does not support pow-
ers of traces of rational expressions, and thus cannot cur-
rently asymptotically solve the Harish-Chandra-Itzykson-
Zuber integral (Troiani et al., 2022).
With regards to future functionality, auto-fpt can be
developed to work with non-Gaussian random matrices
and non-commuting deterministic matrices.  In addition,
auto-fpt can be developed to provide more explicit sup-
port for converting the objectives of classiп¬Ғcation problems
into rational expressions, and support for learning problems
where a closed-form solution may not exist. Furthermore,
8

Calculations for Machine Learning Theory
it can currently require some manual effort to rewrite inter-
esting quantities in ML as the expected trace of a rational
expression, which is required to compute a pencil and use
auto-fpt.  Therefore, auto-fpt can be extended to
intelligently perform this rewriting. auto-fpt can also
be extended to: (1) manipulate and automatically solve the
system of equations output by the program, and (2) pro-
duce phase diagrams for quantities of interest. In addition,
auto-fpt may require further development to be inte-
grated as a subroutine into proof assistants.
Broader Impacts Statement
We hope that auto-fpt makes the machinery of ML
theory more accessible to theorists and practitioners, and
can help reproduce known and uncover new phenomena
in ML that contribute to our foundational understanding
of sociotechnical phenomena like robustness and fairness.
auto-fpt should be used in conjunction with real-world
experiments to avoid overclaiming aspects of ML models.
auto-fpt has potential to be used in educational settings
to teach RMT.
